m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the process of defining more than one method in a class differentiated by method signature?
m_Item1.Explanation: Method overloading is multiple methods with different signature.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is polymorphism in Java?

Options.OptionText: The ability of different classes to be treated as instances of the same class
The ability of a class to have multiple constructors
The ability of a method to be overridden in a subclass
The ability of a class to inherit from multiple classes

m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated as objects of a common superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of polymorphism?
m_Item1.Explanation: Method overloading allows different methods to have the same name with different parameters, a form of polymorphism.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used in Java to achieve dynamic polymorphism?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'override' keyword is used when a child class needs to provide a specific implementation for a method that is already defined in its parent class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) determine which method to invoke at runtime?

Options.OptionText: Based on the type of object

m_Item1.Explanation: JVM decides which method to execute based on the type of the actual object (not the reference variable type), leading to dynamic method dispatch.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main advantage of polymorphism?
m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism enhances program flexibility and code reusability by allowing one interface to accommodate different underlying forms.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a type of polymorphism in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Type conversion is not considered polymorphism. Polymorphism categories include compile-time (method overloading) and runtime (method overriding).

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is runtime polymorphism achieved in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Runtime polymorphism is achieved through method overriding, where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method declared in its superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which situation can polymorphism be used?

Options.OptionText: When an object reference can refer to different types
To define a fixed set of operations

m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism is used when an object holds various forms — references of type superclass used to hold objects of subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What role does inheritance play in polymorphism?

Options.OptionText: It is a prerequisite for polymorphism

m_Item1.Explanation: Inheritance allows for method overriding, a key component of achieving polymorphism through dynamic method dispatch.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about polymorphism?

Options.OptionText: Polymorphism decisions are made at compile time
Polymorphism allows for one interface to suit various forms
Polymorphism does not work with abstract classes

m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism truly allows an interface to serve multiple forms, making the system more flexible and reusable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is method overloading in Java?

Options.OptionText: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters
Defining a method with no return type
Using the static keyword in a method

m_Item1.Explanation: Method overloading allows multiple methods in the same class to have the same name with different parameter lists.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can static methods be overridden to achieve polymorphism?

Options.OptionText: No, static methods belong to the class

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods are not subject to polymorphic behaviors because they belong to the class, not instances of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which access modifier should methods have to allow achieving polymorphism through overriding?
m_Item1.Explanation: To allow overriding, methods should be declared with protected, public, or package-private access, but not private.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does method overriding enable in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Method overriding allows a subclass to provide a specific implementation of a method declared in its superclass, enabling dynamic polymorphism.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does polymorphism contribute to open/closed principle?

Options.OptionText: By making code easy to debug
By allowing methods to be changed
By enabling new functionality using existing code without modification

m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism supports extending functionality by using existing interfaces, promoting open/closed principle: open for extension, closed for modification.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is false about method overriding?

Options.OptionText: Method signature must be the same in parent and child class
Return type must be of same or subtype
It is a form of compile-time polymorphism
Access level cannot be more restrictive in child class

m_Item1.Explanation: Method overriding is a form of runtime polymorphism, not compile-time. It resolves which method to execute at runtime.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these interfaces can be used in achieving polymorphism?
m_Item1.Explanation: Runnable provides a single method signature (run) that can be implemented differently across various classes to achieve polymorphism.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which concept in Java allows a superclass reference to point to a subclass object?
m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism permits a superclass reference variable to point to subclass objects, a key feature of dynamic method dispatch.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What can restrict polymorphic behavior?
m_Item1.Explanation: Declaring a class or method as final restricts further inheritance or overriding, limiting polymorphic behavior.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about polymorphism is true?

Options.OptionText: Polymorphism increases the performance at runtime
Polymorphism aims to perform a single action in different ways
Polymorphic variables are resolved at compile time

m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism is about ability to perform a single action in many forms, often achieved through dynamic method dispatch at runtime.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Java, what is typecasting?

Options.OptionText: Converting a variable from one type to another.
Defining methods with the same name.
Creating objects from the blueprint of a class.
Inheriting features of one class into another.

m_Item1.Explanation: Typecasting is the process of converting a variable from one data type to another in Java.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of explicit typecasting in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Explicit typecasting in Java is done manually by placing the type in parentheses before the value, as shown in option C.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is used in Java for ensuring type safety when performing typecasting?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'instanceof' keyword is used in Java to check whether an object is an instance of a specific class or interface, ensuring type safety before casting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the output of the following code? int a = 5; double b = a; System.out.println(b);
m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, an 'int' can be implicitly cast to a 'double', so the value of 'a' is promoted to a double, resulting in output '5.0'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which Java operator is used for explicit typecasting?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, typecasting is done explicitly by specifying the target type in parentheses before the value, as shown in option B.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these scenarios will require explicit typecasting in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Explicit typecasting is required when converting from a larger type to a smaller type, such as from 'double' to 'int'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the risk associated with explicit typecasting in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Explicit typecasting can lead to data loss when converting from a type with a larger range (such as 'double') to a type with a smaller range (such as 'int').

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will the following code print? char c = 'A'; int ascii = (int)c; System.out.println(ascii);
m_Item1.Explanation: The character 'A' has an ASCII value of 65. Casting the char to an int will print this ASCII value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In what situation would a ClassCastException be thrown?

Options.OptionText: When casting a superclass reference to an unrelated subclass.
When casting an int to a double.
When casting a byte to an int.
When casting a double to a float.

m_Item1.Explanation: A ClassCastException is thrown when attempting to cast an object to a class of which it is not an instance, such as casting a superclass reference to an unrelated subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method below properly uses polymorphism with typecasting?
m_Item1.Explanation: Polymorphism allows an object of a subclass to be referenced by a superclass type. Option B casts a Dog object to its superclass type Animal, showcasing polymorphic behavior.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following options is correct about the use of 'instanceof' in Java?

Options.OptionText: It checks whether an object is an instance of a specific class or subclass.
It creates a new instance of the specified class.
It returns the class name of the object.
It casts an object to a superclass.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'instanceof' keyword is used to test whether an object is an instance of a particular class or subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given a superclass Animal and a subclass Dog, how would you correctly use 'instanceof'?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'instanceof' operator is used with the instance first, followed by the class reference, to check if the object belongs to the specified class or its subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will the following code return: 'null instanceof MyClass'?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'instanceof' operator returns false if the tested object is null, irrespective of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is a valid use of 'instanceof' keyword for interface implementation?
m_Item1.Explanation: You can use 'instanceof' to check if an object implements an interface by placing the object name first and then the interface name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given: Object obj = new String("hello"); What will return 'obj instanceof String'?
m_Item1.Explanation: Since 'obj' is pointing to an instance of 'String', the 'instanceof' check returns true.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can 'instanceof' be used to check covariant return types?

Options.OptionText: Yes, but only with explicit casting
No, 'instanceof' does not apply to return types

m_Item1.Explanation: 'instanceof' is used for checking whether an object is an instance of a particular class/interface, not for verifying return types.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a valid reason for using 'instanceof'?

Options.OptionText: To determine if an object is null
To check runtime type of an object

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'instanceof' keyword does not determine if an object is null; it simply returns false if the object is null.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When using 'instanceof' with interfaces, what is important to consider?

Options.OptionText: The object must explicitly declare the interface.
The class of the object must implement the interface.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'instanceof' keyword can be used to check if an object implements an interface, provided the object's class actually implements the interface.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why might you use 'instanceof' with a superclass type?

Options.OptionText: To ensure the object is an instance of a subclass
To determine if the object can be cast to a subclass
To check if the object supports certain interface methods

m_Item1.Explanation: Using 'instanceof' with a superclass type helps determine if the object is part of that inheritance hierarchy.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If 'instanceof' check returns true, what can be safely concluded?

Options.OptionText: The object is definitely a superclass of the specified type
The object can be cast to the specified type
The object does not belong to any subclass of the specified type
The specified type implements an interface

m_Item1.Explanation: If 'instanceof' returns true, the object is guaranteed to be an instance of the class/interface specified or a subclass thereof, and thus can be safely cast.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is dynamic method dispatch?

Options.OptionText: Calling a method based on reference type
Calling a method based on object type at runtime

m_Item1.Explanation: Dynamic method dispatch is the process where a call to an overridden method is resolved at runtime based on the actual object type.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output?
class A {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("A");
    }
}
class B extends A {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("B");
    }
}
class C extends B {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("C");
    }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        A obj = new C();
        obj.show();
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The reference is of type A, but the actual object is of type C. Due to dynamic method dispatch, C's show() method is called.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the key difference between method overriding and method hiding in Java?

Options.OptionText: Overriding applies to static methods, hiding to non-static methods
Overriding is resolved at compile time, hiding at runtime
Overriding applies to instance methods, hiding to static methods

m_Item1.Explanation:
Method overriding applies to instance methods and is resolved at runtime using dynamic method dispatch.
Method hiding applies to static methods, and is resolved at compile time based on the reference type.
Static methods cannot be overridden; they can only be hidden if redefined in a subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about method overriding?

Options.OptionText: Overridden method must have a different name
Overridden method must have the same signature

m_Item1.Explanation: Method overriding requires the same method signature (name and parameters) in both superclass and subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a valid use of the instanceof operator?

Options.OptionText: To check if an object belongs to a specific class

m_Item1.Explanation: The instanceof operator checks whether an object is an instance of a specific class or subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
String s = "Hello";
System.out.println(s instanceof Object);
m_Item1.Explanation: String is a subclass of Object, so s instanceof Object returns true.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What happens if you try to downcast an object to an incompatible type?
Object obj = new Integer(10);
String str = (String) obj;
m_Item1.Explanation: This results in a ClassCastException at runtime because obj is not actually a String.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which letters will be printed when the given program is run?
public class MyClass {
public static void main(String[] args) {
B b = new C();
A a = b;
if (a instanceof A)
     System.out.println("A");
if (a instanceof B)
     System.out.println("B");
if (a instanceof C)
    System.out.println("C");
if (a instanceof D)
    System.out.println("D");
}
}
class A {}
class B extends A {}
class C extends B {}
class D extends C {}

Options.OptionText: only A and B will be printed.

m_Item1.Explanation: A has been assigned instance of 'C' which exends from 'B' which finally extends from 'A'. So a is instance of A,B and C but not of D. So it prints A, B and C but not "D".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output  of this program?
class A  {
int i;
int j;
A() {
i = 1;
j = 2;
}
}
class Output {
public static void main(String args[]) {
A obj1  =new  A();
A obj2 =new  A();
System.out.print(obj1.equals(obj2));
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: equals() method is not overridden in class A so it gives a call to equals() method in class Object which compares the references.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will this code print?
int arr[] = new  int [5];
System.out.print(arr);

Options.OptionText: value stored in arr[0].

m_Item1.Explanation: Array object ID gets printed as toString() method is not overloaded for array objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following Java program?
1.   class box
2.   {
3.        int width;
4.        int height;
5.        int length;
6.   }
7.    class mainclass
8.    {
9.        public static void main(String args[])
10.        {        
11.            box obj = new box();
12.            System.out.println(obj);
13.        }
14.    }
m_Item1.Explanation: When we print object internally toString() will be called to return string into this format classname@hashcode in hexadecimal form.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What concepts come under Polymorphism in java?
m_Item1.Explanation:
All mentioned features come under polymorphism in java oop.
•Method overloading (multiple methods with same name in a class)
•Constructor overloading (multiple constructors in a class)
•Method overriding (methods with same in base and derived class)

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which polymorphism concept is applied to inheritance relationship in java programming?
m_Item1.Explanation: Method overriding concept is related with inheritance in java. Both base and derived class contains methods with same name and signature.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In below java code, whose “Car” will be called?
class Father {
    public void car() {
        System.out.println("Father's Car");
    }
}
class Son extends Father {
    public void car() {
        System.out.println("Son's Car");
    }
}
public class Sample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Son john = new Son();
        john.car();
    }
}

Options.OptionText: There is an ambiguity, so no one Car

m_Item1.Explanation:
Since, both Father class and Son class contain car () method, So, on object creation of Son class, the son’s car() method will be called. Son has overrides car method of base class Father.
 Note that if son does not contain car method, then father class car () method will be called.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which method of base class X, the derived class Y cannon override?
class X {    
    final public void m1() {
    }
    public void m2() {
    }
}
class Y extends X {
    // override?
}

Options.OptionText: Both m1() and m2() can override

m_Item1.Explanation: Derive class Y cannot Override base class method m1() as this method is declared as final. Final methods can not be overridden in subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which are true statements regarding polymorphism concept.

Options.OptionText: Method overloading is used in same class only
Constructor overloading is used in same class only
Method overriding is used in base class and derived class

m_Item1.Explanation: Method overloading – multiple methods with same name and different number of arguments or data types are written in the same class only.Similar is with constructor overloading. By the way, constructor of a class in java programming cannot be written in other classes.Method overriding is used in inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is output of following program
class A {
int i;
void display()
{
System.out.println(i);
}
}    
class B extends A {
int j;
void display()
{
System.out.println(j);
}
}    
class inheritance_demo {
public static void main(String args[])
{
B obj = new B();
obj.i=1;
obj.j=2;   
obj.display();     
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: As j variable is not declared as private i,j can be assigned with the value from main method. display() method will be called from class B and it will display value of j i.e. 2

m_Item1.QuestionText: When an overridden method is called from within a subclass, it will always  refer to the version of that method defined by the
m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is correct about passing an argument by call-by-value process?

Options.OptionText: Copy of argument is made into the formal parameter of the subroutine
Reference to original argument is passed to formal parameter of the subroutine
Copy of argument is made into the formal parameter of the subroutine and changes made on parameters of subroutine have effect on original argument
Reference to original argument is passed to formal parameter of the subroutine and changes made on parameters of subroutine have effect on original argument

m_Item1.Explanation: When we pass an argument by call-by-value a copy of argument is made into the formal parameter of the subroutine and changes made on parameters of subroutine have no effect on original argument, they remain the same.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following code?

class San {
    public void m1 (int i,float f) {
        System.out.println(" int float method");
    }
    public void m1(float f,int i) {
        System.out.println("float int method");
   }
   public static void main(String[]args) {
        San s=new San();
        s.m1(20,20);
  }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: While resolving overloaded method call, compiler automatically promotes data types if exact match is not found. But in this case, m1 is called with 2 int values. As int can be promoted to float as float is higher data type but then it will be ambuguity to compiler because 2 methods found matching for this call.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
    class overload    {
        int x;
        int y;
        void add(int a)    {
            x =  a + 1;
        }
        void add(int a, int b)    {
            x =  a + 2;
        }        
    }    
    class Overload_methods    {
        public static void main(String args[])       {
            overload obj = new overload();   
            int a = 0;
            obj.add(6);
            System.out.println(obj.x);     
        }
   }
m_Item1.Explanation:
None.
output:
$ javac Overload_methods.java
$ java Overload_methods
7

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   class overload    {
        int x;
        double y;
        void add(int a , int b)      {
            x = a + b;
        }
        void add(double c , double d)       {
            y = c + d;
        }
        overload()     {
            this.x = 0;
            this.y = 0;
        }        
    }    
    class Overload_methods    {
        public static void main(String args[])      {
            overload obj = new overload();   
            int a = 2;
            double b = 3.2;
            obj.add(a, a);
            obj.add(b, b);
            System.out.println(obj.x + " " + obj.y);     
        }
   }
m_Item1.Explanation:
For obj.add(a,a); ,the function in line number 4 gets executed and value of x is 4. For the next function call, the function in line number 7 gets executed and value of y is 6.4
output:
$ javac Overload_methods.java
$ java Overload_methods
4 6.4

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
    class test  {
        int a;
        int b;
        void meth(int i , int j)   {
            i *= 2;
            j /= 2;
        }          
    }    
    class Output  {
        public static void main(String args[])  {
            test obj = new test();
            int a = 10;
            int b = 20;             
            obj.meth(a , b);
            System.out.println(a + " " + b);        
    }
 }
m_Item1.Explanation:
Variables a & b are passed by value, copy of their values are made on formal parameters of function meth() that is i & j. Therefore changes done on i & j are not reflected back on original arguments. a & b remain 10 & 20 respectively.
output:
$ javac Output.java
$ java Output
10 20

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
    class test  {
        int a;
        int b;
        test(int i, int j)  {
            a = i;
            b = j;
        }
        void meth(test o)   {
            o.a *= 2;
            O.b /= 2;
        }          
    }    
    class Output  {
        public static void main(String args[])
        {
            test obj = new test(10 , 20);
            obj.meth(obj);
            System.out.println(obj.a + " " + obj.b);        
        }
    }
m_Item1.Explanation:
Class objects are always passed by reference, therefore changes done are reflected back on original arguments. obj.meth(obj) sends object obj as parameter whose variables a & b are multiplied and divided by 2 respectively by meth() function of class test. a & b becomes 20 & 10 respectively.
output:
$ javac Output.java
$ java Output
20 10
